Name: spectral_latency_aware_inference
Title: Spectral Latency-Aware Inference for Low-Latency Machine Learning
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, enabling efficient and reliable machine learning in resource-constrained environments.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in training-free machine learning, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation, which have shown the potential of spectral methods for efficient inference. However, our approach differs in that it focuses on latency-aware inference and uses spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable protocols.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to latency-aware inference, leveraging spectral stability bounds to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical machine learning. Our framework, Spectral Latency-Aware Inference (SLAI), uses a spectral decomposition of the activation covariance matrix to identify the dominant spectral directions that govern implicit latent refinement. By projecting test-time updates onto a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace, SLAI ensures strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground SLAI in information-theoretic bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we demonstrate that SLAI matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70% and enforcing deterministic latency bounds.
Experiments: Evaluate SLAI on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, comparing its performance to full-space perturbation baselines.
Measure the reduction in hidden-state perturbation overhead and the enforcement of deterministic latency bounds.
Investigate the impact of spectral decomposition on the optimization landscape's geometry and the resulting inference efficiency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of SLAI may depend on the quality of the spectral decomposition and the choice of hyperparameters.
The approach may not be suitable for all types of machine learning tasks or models.
The theoretical guarantees provided by SLAI may not always translate to empirical improvements in practice.
